elements.sent-2411-001: A widely used method to ensure privacy of unstructured text data is the multidimensional Laplace mechanism for $\dx$-privacy, which is a relaxation of differential privacy for metric spaces.
elements.sent-2411-002: We identify an intriguing peculiarity of this mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-003: When applied on a word-by-word basis, the mechanism either outputs the original word, or completely dissimilar words, and very rarely any semantically similar words.
elements.sent-2411-004: We investigate this observation in detail, and tie it to the fact that the distance of the nearest neighbor of a word in any word embedding model (which are high-dimensional) is much larger than the relative difference in distances to any of its two consecutive neighbors.
elements.sent-2411-005: We also show that the dot product of the multidimensional Laplace noise vector with any word embedding plays a crucial role in designating the nearest neighbor.
elements.sent-2411-006: We derive the distribution, moments and tail bounds of this dot product.
elements.sent-2411-007: We further propose a fix as a post-processing step, which satisfactorily removes the above-mentioned issue.
elements.sent-2411-008: Unstructured text is the most common method of communication in the real-world, in the form of emails, sharing reports, and entering prompts to generative artificial intelligence (AI) models to quote a more contemporary example.
elements.sent-2411-009: In many scenarios, part of the text needs to be sanitized due to privacy reasons.
elements.sent-2411-010: Examples include authorship anonymization to protect identities of whistleblowers, redacting sensitive information before releasing documents as part of freedom of information requests, and ensuring that user submitted prompts to a third-party generative AI model does not contain sensitive company information.
elements.sent-2411-011: With the increasing use of differential privacy~\cite{dwork2006calibrating as a principled way of releasing data with privacy in many real-world applications, it has also been proposed as an automated way to achieve privacy in the text domain.}
elements.sent-2411-012: More specifically, a generalization of differential privacy to metric spaces, called $\dx$-privacy~\cite{chatzikokolakis2013broadening , has been proposed as a method to sanitize sensitive text~\cite{fernandes2019generalised, feyisetan2020dxprivacy, qu2021bert, yue2021dxprivacy, li2023llm-prompt, mattern-limits-word-level-dp .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Informally $\dx$-privacy ensures that it is harder to distinguish objects in a metric space that are closer to one another under the distance metric of the metric space than objects further away.
elements.sent-2411-014: This promises better utility than ordinary differential privacy as in many use cases it suffices to provide privacy up to a certain granularity.
elements.sent-2411-015: An analogy is location data; disclosing the city or postcode one resides in is less of a concern than the exact street address.
elements.sent-2411-016: There are a number of ways in which $\dx$-privacy can be used to santize text data.
elements.sent-2411-017: One common method is word-by-word sanitization through the word-level multidimensional Laplace mechanism for $\dx$-privacy, applied in the following manner~\cite{fernandes2019generalised, feyisetan2020dxprivacy, qu2021bert, yue2021dxprivacy, li2023llm-prompt .}
elements.sent-2411-018: We assume a pre-trained machine learning model is available which vectorizes words, i.e., converts them into embeddings in a high-dimensional space.
elements.sent-2411-019: This pre-trained model is public information.
elements.sent-2411-020: The words form the vocabulary and the corresponding embeddings, the embedding space.
elements.sent-2411-021: Given a word in a sentence to be sanitized, a noise vector is generated calibrated according to the privacy parameter $\epsilon$, and then added to the word embedding resulting in a vector in the embedding space.
elements.sent-2411-022: Almost always, this does not correspond to the embedding of any word in the vocabulary of the embedding model.
elements.sent-2411-023: We can instead find the nearest neighbor (e.g., with respect to the Euclidean distance) to this noisy embedding in the embedding space, and output the resulting word.
elements.sent-2411-024: This method can be applied independently to each word, and the complete sentence can then be used as the sanitized text.
elements.sent-2411-025: When sanitizing text through this method we encountered a confounding \ifisfullpaper observation~\cite{robin2024santizellm .. \else observation.}
elements.sent-2411-026: However, we observed that through the entire spectrum of values of $\epsilon$, the mechanism almost always outputs either the original word or words which are very far off in distance and semantic similarity to the original word.
elements.sent-2411-027: The nearest neighbors of the original word are seldom output by the \ifisfullpaper mechanism~\cite{robin2024santizellm .. \else mechanism.}
elements.sent-2411-028: However, this is not true in this instance, or not true in the way we may think, since the word embedding models are trained to ensure that the Euclidean distance is an effective metric to identify similar words in the embedding space~\cite{glove, word2vec .}
elements.sent-2411-029: See Section~\ref{sec:rw for a detailed discussion on this topic.}
elements.sent-2411-030: In this paper, we investigate this observation in detail and identify the post-processing step of the nearest neighbor search of the perturbed embedding in a high dimensional space as the culprit.
elements.sent-2411-031: Along the way, we unravel a number of other contributions which we believe will prove useful to construct better $\dx$-privacy mechanisms for text data.
elements.sent-2411-032: The multidimensional Laplace mechanism is fundamental to $\dx$-privacy due to its ease of implementation, and hence wide-spread use, akin to the Laplace mechanism~\cite{dwork2006calibrating for ordinary differential privacy.}
elements.sent-2411-033: We highlight the above-mentioned issue in a very commonly used $\dx$-privacy mechanism for text data~\cite{fernandes2019generalised, feyisetan2020dxprivacy, qu2021bert, yue2021dxprivacy, li2023llm-prompt, mattern-limits-word-level-dp , whereby across different values of $\epsilon$ and different word embedding models, almost always either the word is not replaced, or is replaced by a completely dissimilar word.}
elements.sent-2411-034: While analyzing the above phenomenon it turns out that the dot product of the noise vector against any word embedding plays a crucial role.
elements.sent-2411-035: This distribution, which we call the noisy dot product distribution, has a length component, which is known to follow the gamma distribution~\cite{feyisetan2020dxprivacy , and an angular component.}
elements.sent-2411-036: We derive the probability density function of the angular component and its moments.
elements.sent-2411-037: We show that the angular component is sub-Gaussian with variance $1/n$, where $n$ is the number of dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-038: This means that the cosine of the angle of the noise vector with any embedding is within $\mathcal{O (1/\sqrt{n )$, and hence the noise vector is increasingly orthogonal to any embedding regardless of the distribution of words in the embedding model.}}
elements.sent-2411-039: We further prove tail-bounds on the noisy dot product distribution showing that its mass is concentrated within $\mathcal{O (\sqrt{n /\epsilon)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-040: Through our analysis, we show that the aforementioned observation is related to the fact that in high dimensions the nearest neighbor of a word is more distant than the relative difference of the distances of its two nearest neighbors.
elements.sent-2411-041: We prove necessary conditions on the initial word to be output by the mechanism as opposed to its close neighbors by relating it to the noisy dot product distribution.
elements.sent-2411-042: We propose a possible mitigation as a further post-processing step, and show that the resulting mechanism gives better utility and behaves as expected.
elements.sent-2411-043: The advantage of our proposed fix is that it does not amend the original mechanism, and only adds an extra step.
elements.sent-2411-044: We release the code of our experiments, including this fix, to promote reproducibility\footnote{Anonymous repo: \url{https://github.com/ticampkietalw/dx-privacy-text .}}

elements.sent-2411-128: In recent years, a number of machine learning models have sprung up which produce vector representations of words, which we call word embeddings for short.
elements.sent-2411-129: These pre-trained word embeddings can be downloaded and used for natural language processing tasks.
elements.sent-2411-131: These word embeddings lie on a high-dimensional real vector space with dimensions ranging from 50 to 300, and even higher.
elements.sent-2411-132: Some embeddings, such as \verb+GloVe+, come in different dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-133: Despite the high-dimensional space the word embeddings maintain distance-based semantic similarities.
elements.sent-2411-134: In other words, the Euclidean distance is an effective method to obtain nearest neighbors of a word in terms of semantic similarity to the target word.\footnote{See \url{https://nlp.stanford.edu/projects/glove/ .}}
elements.sent-2411-135: Furthermore, these embeddings maintain linear relationships between words, e.g., king minus queen equals man minus woman~\cite{glove .}
elements.sent-2411-136: For a more detailed introduction to vector representation of words and models producing word embeddings, see~\cite[\S 15]{dive-deep-book .}

elements.sent-2411-447: When applied on a word-by-word basis, the mechanism either outputs the original word, or completely dissimilar words, and very rarely outputs semantically similar words.
elements.sent-2411-448: Examples include authorship anonymization to protect identities of whistleblowers, redacting sensitive information before releasing documents as part of freedom of information requests, and ensuring that user submitted prompts to a third-party generative AI model do not contain sensitive company information.
elements.sent-2411-449: More specifically, a generalization of differential privacy to metric spaces, called $\dx$-privacy~\cite{chatzikokolakis2013broadening , has been proposed as a method to sanitize sensitive text~\cite{fernandes2019generalised, feyisetan2020dxprivacy, qu2021bert, yue2021dxprivacy, li2023llm-prompt, mattern-limits-word-level-dp .\footnote{$\dx$-privacy is also commonly known as metric privacy.}}}
elements.sent-2411-450: The words form the vocabulary, and their corresponding embeddings define the embedding space.
elements.sent-2411-451: This method can be applied independently to each word, and the complete sentence can then be used as the sanitized text.\footnote{Apart from this use case, this mechanism has also been used for author obfuscation~\cite{fernandes2019generalised , and to provide privacy where each user's input is a set of one or more words~\cite{feyisetan2020dxprivacy .}}}
elements.sent-2411-452: When sanitizing text through this method we encountered a confounding observation~\cite{robin2024santizellm .}
elements.sent-2411-453: If this method is applied several times on the same word, one expects to see the original word, followed by its closest neighbors as the most frequent words output by the mechanism, with the frequency dropping smoothly but exponentially as we move away from the original word.
elements.sent-2411-454: The nearest neighbors of the original word are seldom output by the mechanism~\cite{robin2024santizellm .}

elements.sent-2411-500: The fact that $\dx$-privacy, in particular, the multidimensional Laplace mechanism does not provide adequate protection for isolated points in the input domain has also been highlighted in the case of location privacy~\cite{chatzikokolakis2015elastic, biswas2023privic .}
elements.sent-2411-501: We discuss here why we think these solutions are not readily applicable to fix our issue in a high-dimensional space.
elements.sent-2411-502: The works in~\cite{chatzikokolakis2015elastic, biswas2023privic use the concept of elastic distinguishability introduced in~\cite{chatzikokolakis2015elastic .}}
elements.sent-2411-503: The idea is that in addition to the usual requirement in $\dx$-privacy that nearby points should have higher probability of being sampled than far away points, the mechanism should also sample a point proportional to its probability mass or density.
elements.sent-2411-504: For instance, an isolated location such as an island has a lower probability mass than a location in a dense urban area~\cite{chatzikokolakis2015elastic .}
elements.sent-2411-505: The mechanisms in~\cite{chatzikokolakis2015elastic, biswas2023privic are essentially weighted versions of the exponential mechanism, where the probability of sampling a point is also weighted by its probability mass.}
elements.sent-2411-506: Location has a natural candidate for assigning a probability mass to points in space: the number of people at a given location.
elements.sent-2411-507: First, we emphasize that their observation is related to the (location) data distribution, with the assumption that the bulk of data is concentrated with only a few isolated points.
elements.sent-2411-508: This is unlike our finding where we show that in essence every embedding in high dimensions is isolated.
elements.sent-2411-509: Second, in the context of location data, even isolated points have a non-zero probability of being output by the mechanism, as they are valid locations.
elements.sent-2411-510: Consequently, there is no need for a nearest neighbor search.
elements.sent-2411-511: Indeed the mechanisms in ~\cite{chatzikokolakis2015elastic, biswas2023privic do not use it.}
elements.sent-2411-512: This is unlike the word embedding space, where a random vector is overwhelmingly not an actual word embedding, and hence the need to perform the nearest neighbor search which, as we saw, is what causes the issue identified in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-513: Third, if we were to apply a similar mechanism to our setting, we need to determine how to assign probability mass to regions in the embedding space.
elements.sent-2411-514: One way to do this is to divide the embedding space into equal-volume hypercubes, count the number of embeddings falling in each hypercube and assign the fractional count as the probability mass.
elements.sent-2411-515: This is analogous to how the two-dimensional location map is divided into a grid in~\cite{chatzikokolakis2015elastic, biswas2023privic .}
elements.sent-2411-516: However, dividing the embedding space in hypercubes is not trivial.
elements.sent-2411-517: On the one hand, dividing each of the $n$ dimensions into $m$ shares results in $m^n$ hypercubes which is unfit for storage (e.g., $n=300$ in some of our vocabularies).
elements.sent-2411-518: On the other hand, choosing a subset of the $n$ dimensions to be divided requires an analysis of which dimension to divide or not.
elements.sent-2411-519: Thus it is unclear whether the notion of elastic distinguishability offers a solution let alone a feasible one.
elements.sent-2411-520: The work in~\cite{athanasiou2025enhancingmetric also looks at the distinguishability of isolated points in the shuffle model of differential privacy.}
elements.sent-2411-521: In the shuffle model, an intermediate shuffler permutes (local) differentially private inputs of end users before submitting them to the central aggregator.
elements.sent-2411-522: This amplifies privacy compared to the local model as the server cannot tie an output to a particular user.
elements.sent-2411-523: In the $\dx$-privacy equivalent version of the shuffle model, the server can still identify isolated inputs (even when perturbed) as they stand out from the rest.
elements.sent-2411-524: To remove this drawback, one of the techniques proposed in~\cite{athanasiou2025enhancingmetric is to encode an input in unary and then apply differentially private noise (randomized response) to each bit before sending it to the shuffler.}
elements.sent-2411-525: It is not clear how these techniques can be applied to the case of text embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-526: We would need to convert embeddings into bit vectors and then combine multiple embeddings together.
elements.sent-2411-527: Moreover, once again, the isolated point issue is endemic in high dimensional embeddings as opposed to being just a few bad apples.
elements.sent-2411-528: More specifically, it almost never outputs semantically related words, as it is expected to do for utility.
elements.sent-2411-529: Instead, we have identified the post-processing step of the nearest neighbor search as the culprit, causing each word embedding to behave as an outlier in high dimensions.
